title: Lecture 17: Mass Wasting and Indian Landslides

content_sections.title: Fundamentals of Mass Wasting and Indian Landslides

blocks.text: In the previous topic, we established that weathering is the static disintegration and decomposition of rocks in situ. This directly explains mass wasting because the products of weathering, weakened and loosened by chemical and physical processes, become susceptible to the downward movement under the direct influence of gravity. Mass wasting is the geomorphic process by which soil, sand, regolith, and rock move downslope as a solid, plastic, or liquid mass.

Mass wasting processes occur when the gravitational force acting on a slope exceeds the shear strength of the material composing the slope. Factors reducing shear strength include saturation by water (which increases pore water pressure), removal of lateral support (by erosion or human excavation), and oversteepening of slopes. Unlike erosion, mass wasting does not require a transporting medium such as water, wind, or ice, though water often acts as a lubricant.

Landslides represent a specific, rapid form of mass wasting involving the collapse of a mass of earth or rock from a high cliff or slope. In the Indian context, the Himalayas are the most landslide-prone zone due to their youth, tectonic instability, and the prevalence of unconsolidated sedimentary deposits. Frequent seismic activity along the plate boundary continuously destabilizes the mountain slopes, triggering massive debris flows.

Beyond the Himalayas, landslide susceptibility is high in the North-eastern hill ranges, the Western Ghats, the Nilgiris, the Eastern Ghats, and the Vindhyas. These regions face varying degrees of risk depending on rainfall intensity, slope gradient, and land-use patterns. Anthropogenic pressures, including large-scale infrastructure development like dams and road construction, significantly heighten the frequency of these disasters.

This understanding of the geomorphic and anthropogenic drivers of mass movement becomes critical when we study Disaster Management, as it forms the foundational basis for hazard zonation mapping and the formulation of slope stabilization policies in India.

content_sections.title: Advanced Analysis — Mass Wasting and Indian Landslides

blocks.text: The Himalayan susceptibility is exacerbated by its complex geological structure. The mountain chain is composed largely of sedimentary rocks, often folded and faulted, which act as weak planes. Heavy snowfall and subsequent snowmelt cycles in the spring induce high pore-water pressure, leading to debris avalanches. In contrast, the Western Ghats experience landslides primarily due to high-intensity monsoon rainfall on steep, weathered slopes, whereas the eastern side of the peninsula, characterized by gentler slopes and rivers in a senile stage, displays higher stability.

Anthropogenic drivers are now primary catalysts for slope failure. In the North-eastern states, the practice of Jhumming (shifting cultivation) destabilizes topsoil on steep inclines, facilitating large-scale denudation. Similarly, in the Western Ghats and Himalayan foothills, indiscriminate road widening and the construction of massive hydroelectric projects disrupt the natural angle of repose, leading to recurrent slope failures.

Mitigation strategies necessitate an area-specific approach. Hazard zonation mapping serves as the primary tool to identify high-vulnerability regions. Structural interventions include the construction of retaining walls, check dams to reduce kinetic energy of runoff, and extensive afforestation to bind the soil matrix. Non-structural measures involve enforcing strict land-use regulations, restricting large settlements in vulnerable zones, and integrating real-time landslide early warning systems (LEWS) in critical corridors.

content_sections.title: NCERT Reference — Mass Wasting and Indian Landslides

blocks.text: Source: NCERT Class 11, 'Fundamentals of Physical Geography', Chapter 6 (Geomorphic Processes). The NCERT classifies mass movements as slow (creep, solifluction) or rapid (earthflow, mudflow, landslide, slump). Landslides are defined as the rapid and perceptible movement of rock and soil.
• Landslides occur when the shear stress of the slope exceeds the shear strength of the materials.
• Gravity is the primary force, but water acts as a trigger by adding weight and reducing internal friction.
• Vegetation cover acts as a stabilizer; removing it through deforestation or overgrazing increases the rate of mass movement.
• The distinction between soil creep (very slow) and landslides (rapid) is essential for identifying geomorphic risks.

1. Confusion between Erosion and Mass Wasting: UPSC often conflates these in options. Remember, erosion requires an external agent (water, ice, wind) to transport material, while mass wasting is primarily gravity-driven, moving the bulk mass of material downslope.

2. Regional Vulnerability: A common trap is assuming the entire Peninsular region is stable. Candidates must note that while the Deccan shield is stable, the Western Ghats are a high-risk zone for landslides, not just the Himalayas.

3. Triggering Mechanisms: Students often overlook that earthquakes (tectonic activity) in the Himalayas do not just cause tremors; they act as a trigger for mass wasting by loosening the geological structure. Linking seismicity to mass wasting is a frequent UPSC requirement.

Forward Hook: This foundational knowledge of geomorphic processes is essential for the next unit on 'Drainage Systems', where we will observe how mass wasting debris influences the formation of river terraces and potential dam-burst flood scenarios.
